title: Chapter 29: Plan B
content: The text arrived at 11:47 PM Pacific.
TRANSMITTERS BURNED. PLAN B.

Chloe Park read it once, set her phone face-down on the desk, and took a slow sip of the warm Monster Ultra at her elbow. The aftertaste was chemical and faintly citrus, the flavor of every all-nighter she'd pulled since this thing started. She didn't panic. She didn't swear. She didn't even change her breathing.
She'd been expecting this.

The rented workspace on Telegraph Avenue smelled like solder flux, heated plastic, and the particular burnt-coffee stench of electronics running too hot for too long. Three monitors threw blue-white light across her face — code on the left, network topology on the right, and in the center, the Lumina app's update server architecture, laid bare like a dissected frog. Cables snaked across every surface: HDMI, USB-C, ethernet, a nest of copper and fiber that would have given a fire marshal a cardiac event. Empty energy drink cans lined the windowsill like a soldier's cartridge casings.
Chloe cracked her knuckles. The keyboard was warm under her fingers, the keys worn smooth from weeks of sustained assault. She picked up her phone and typed back two words.
ALREADY RUNNING. TRUST ME.
Then she turned to the center monitor and got to work.
"Never have one plan when you can have two."

She'd said that to Maya on a rooftop in the Mission, three months ago, when this was still theoretical and Maya still laughed at her paranoia. Maya had called her doomscrolling. Chloe had called it preparedness. Same thing, different framing.

The transmitters had always been Plan A. The obvious approach. Hide a few signal-broadcast nodes in the AV infrastructure at the NYSE, piggyback on the live IPO broadcast, inject the evidence into the feed when Vlad was standing at the podium congratulating himself. Elegant. Clean. Direct.
Too direct.

Chloe had known it from the start. The transmitters were a knife to the throat — dramatic, effective, and absolutely the kind of thing a predator would see coming. Vlad Kozlov didn't survive three decades in the gray space between oligarchy and Silicon Valley by missing direct threats. He'd find them. Of course he'd find them. That was the whole point.
Plan A was the decoy. Plan A was the thing Vlad was supposed to find.
Plan B was the thing he'd never look for, because it was already his.
The Lumina app.

Four point two million women had it installed on their phones. A "wellness companion" — period tracking, fertility windows, meditation prompts, sleep coaching — wrapped in pastel gradients and soft typography that whispered self-care. Aeterna Health's crown jewel. The data harvester that Vlad had built his empire on, disguised as a friend.
And every single one of those four point two million phones was connected to the same update server.

Chloe had reverse-engineered the app's update mechanism six days ago. It had taken her eleven hours of continuous work, two pots of coffee, and one moment of genuine awe at how sloppy Lumina's security was. The update protocol was HTTPS, sure — encrypt-in-transit, industry standard, check the box — but the payload verification was a joke. A single SHA-256 hash checked against a CDN manifest that hadn't been rotated in fourteen months. Fourteen months. Aeterna's security team had been too busy prepping for the IPO to rotate their manifests. They'd left the barn door open and gone to celebrate.
So Chloe walked through it.

The backdoor she'd built was surgical. It didn't touch the app's core functionality — it couldn't, or users would notice. It didn't change the icon, alter the navigation, or trigger any permission requests. It just added one tiny conditional branch to the update payload: if server flag == TRUE, load alternate content package from buffer. That was it. A single if-statement, invisible unless you were specifically looking for it, and nobody would be looking because this was their own server, their own app, their own infrastructure.

On IPO day, when Vlad was standing on the NYSE podium, and Lumina's update server pushed its scheduled wellness notification to four point two million phones — "Your cycle starts in 3 days 💙" or "Time for your evening meditation 🌙" — Chloe's flag would flip.
And instead of a gentle reminder, four point two million screens would display the evidence.

The real evidence. The data Vlad had harvested from Lumina users — sold, repackaged, and monetified without consent. The offshore accounts. The clinical trial manipulation. The women whose private health data had been packaged and sold to insurance companies, to employers, to data brokers who traded in human vulnerability. Everything Maya had found, everything Chloe had verified, everything Vlad had buried — it would all appear on four point two million phones simultaneously.
No PR team in the world could contain that.

Chloe stared at the center monitor, watching the update payload compile. The code scrolled in neat green lines, each character a tiny soldier falling into formation. The warmth of the monitors pressed against her face like a fever. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groaned — old Oakland infrastructure, older than the tech bros who'd colonized it.

"If I were Vlad, I'd find the transmitters too," she murmured to the empty room. Her voice sounded strange — she hadn't spoken aloud in hours. "He thinks like a predator. Predators look for the direct attack. They don't look for the thing they already own turning against them."
The Lumina app was Vlad's own weapon. And Chloe was going to use it against him.
She allowed herself one small, sharp smile. Then she picked up her phone and called Jordi.
He answered on the second ring, which meant he'd been waiting.
"Talk to me."

"Transmitters are burned," Chloe said. No preamble. No pleasantries. That was the nice thing about Jordi — he operated at the same clock speed she did. "Vlad found them. Maya's on the move. You know all of this already."
"I know she texted you. I know Vlad used the gala as a trap. I know the FBI's fifteen minutes away from a warrant." A beat. "What I don't know is how we're still in this."
"Plan B," Chloe said. "It's already running. Has been for two weeks."
Silence on the line. Then: "Define 'Plan B.'"
"I'm inside Lumina's update server. Full payload access. On IPO day, I can push the evidence to every phone that has the app installed. Four point two million screens. Simultaneously."
She heard Jordi breathe out — not a sigh, more like a pressure release. "You've been holding out on us."
"I've been being careful. There's a difference."
"Maya didn't know?"

"Maya knew I had contingencies. She didn't need the specifics. Operational security — the fewer people who know a thing, the longer the thing stays true." Chloe swiveled her chair, cables brushing against her ankle. "But there's a problem."
"Of course there is."

"Lumina gets the evidence to the world. Phones. Social media. Screenshots that people take and share before Aeterna can push a kill switch. It's viral by design — impossible to suppress once it's out." She paused. "But it doesn't hit the NYSE."
Another silence. Longer this time.
"Explain."

"Vlad is going to be standing on the NYSE podium. Surrounded by traders, cameras, the whole spectacle. The Lumina push goes to phones — but it doesn't go to the NYSE's internal screens. It doesn't go to the Bloomberg terminal feed. It doesn't go to the live broadcast that every financial network is carrying. Vlad's going to see the phone alerts, sure. But he's also going to see the screens behind him still showing his face, still showing the AET ticker climbing, still showing the world that he's winning. He'll have a narrative: 'hacking incident,' 'misinformation,' 'we're investigating.' He'll spin it. He's good at spinning."
"And if the NYSE screens show the evidence at the same time—"

"Then there's nowhere to spin. The podium where he's celebrating becomes the stage where he's exposed. The world sees it on their phones AND Vlad sees it on the screen right behind his head. Simultaneous. Unspinnable. That's the True Climax."
She could almost hear Jordi thinking — the gears turning, the tactical calculus running. He was good at this. A natural field operator. But even he couldn't be in two places at once.

"So we need someone inside the NYSE," he said slowly. "Someone who can get into the production truck and inject directly into the video feed. Not wireless — they'll be scanning for that after the transmitter discovery. Hard line. Physical cable. Someone walks up, plugs in, and—"
"And the world changes," Chloe finished. "Yeah."
"Maya was supposed to be inside."

"Maya is being hunted by the FBI. She can't go near the NYSE. Every federal agent in Manhattan is going to be watching for her. Vlad probably has his own people looking too. She walks within three blocks of Wall Street, she's done."
"Then who?"

Chloe heard the question she'd been dreading and expecting in equal measure. The same question she'd been asking herself for the last ninety seconds, ever since she'd set her phone down and realized that Plan B had just become Plan A.
The answer was obvious. It had been obvious since the text came in. She just didn't like it.
"Me," she said.
A pause.
"You're in Oakland," Jordi said, as if geography were the primary objection.
"And I can be in New York in six hours. Red-eye out of SFO. Lands at Newark at six AM."
"Chloe. You don't do field work."
"I'm aware."
"You've never—"

"I'm aware, Jordi." She pressed her thumb against her temple. The warmth of the monitors felt farther away now, replaced by the cool certainty of what she was about to do. "I can forge press credentials in four hours. International tech journal — 'Digital Frontiers.' I already have the template. I built it two weeks ago, same time I started on Lumina. I'll have a badge, a press kit, a byline history. It'll hold up at a security checkpoint."
"Two weeks ago," Jordi repeated. "You built press credentials two weeks ago."

"I built everything two weeks ago. The Lumina backdoor, the press badge, the hardline injection protocol. Plan B isn't something I threw together tonight, Jordi. It's something I've been building in the dark while everyone else was focused on the transmitters. While Vlad was looking for the knife, I was building the bomb."
She heard him exhale — slow, measured, the breath of a man who'd just realized he'd been working alongside someone far more dangerous than he'd understood.
"The hardline injection," he said. "You have the hardware?"

"Portable server. Three laptops. Injection cables — SDI to HDMI, compatible with standard production truck setups. I've mapped the NYSE's AV contractor. They use NEP Group, same as every major live event. Their trucks have external video input panels on the port side, standard BNC connectors. I plug in, I push the payload, and the screens inside the exchange show our evidence at the exact same time four point two million phones light up."
"And if you get caught?"

Chloe looked at the dead man's switch app on her phone — the one she'd built the same night as everything else. If her heart rate dropped to zero, if her phone was confiscated, if she didn't check in at the scheduled intervals, the full Lumina payload would auto-deploy. Not on IPO day. Immediately. Suboptimal timing, but still devastating. The insurance policy beneath the insurance policy.
"Then I get caught," she said. "And the dead man's switch deploys anyway. There's no version of this where Vlad wins, Jordi. There's only versions where we win louder or quieter."
A long breath. Then: "When does your flight leave?"
"1:40 AM. I've got a ride to SFO in fifteen minutes."
"I'll meet you at Newark. We'll do final prep on the ground."
"Jordi." Chloe's voice softened — a rare thing, and she could feel the rarity of it like a splinter. "Don't tell Maya I'm coming. Not yet. She'll try to stop me."
"She'll find out."
"Let her find out when I'm already in the air."
A beat. "You're stubborn."
"I'm prepared. Same thing, different framing."
Jordi made a sound that might have been a laugh. "Call me when you land."
The line went dead.
Chloe packed in four minutes flat.

She'd rehearsed this. Not physically — she hadn't known, consciously, that she would ever need to execute this particular maneuver. But her hands knew. Her hands had always known. They moved with the efficient precision of a surgeon closing, each item slotted into the reinforced backpack in its designated pocket.

Laptop one: the Lumina server access rig, already configured, already tested. Laptop two: the injection payload, pre-compiled, ready to push. Laptop three: backup, because you always brought a backup. Portable server: the size of a hardcover book, weighing three pounds, containing enough processing power to hijack a live broadcast feed and maintain it for six minutes — long enough. Hardline injection cables: coiled tight, BNC to SDI, SDI to HDMI, adapters for every port configuration a production truck might throw at her. These she wrapped in a soft cloth, the way a soldier wraps a clean weapon.

The fake press badge went into the interior pocket, against her ribs. She felt its weight there — the laminated card with a photograph she'd taken six months ago, the name of a tech journal that existed only as a domain registration and a shell website. "Digital Frontiers." Senior Technology Correspondent: C. Park. Even Vlad's security consultants would have a hard time disproving it in the twelve seconds they'd have to check.

The dead man's switch app stared up at her from her phone screen. She verified the settings — heart rate monitor active, check-in interval set to ninety minutes, auto-deploy threshold confirmed. She touched the screen once, feeling the cold glass under her fingertip.
Insurance beneath insurance.
She called Maya on the encrypted line. It rang twice.
"Chloe." Maya's voice was tight, controlled — the voice of someone running on adrenaline and discipline through the dark streets of a city that had turned hostile. "Tell me Plan B is real."

"Plan B is real. It's been real for two weeks." Chloe moved through the workspace, killing monitors, pulling drives. "The Lumina app is our Trojan horse. I'm inside the update server — full payload access. On IPO day, I flip a switch and four point two million phones display the evidence. Every Lumina user. Every screenshot. Every share. When that many screens light up with the truth at the same time, no PR team in the world can contain it."
She heard Maya breathe — the rhythm of someone processing a lifeline. "And the NYSE screens?"
"I'll handle it. In person. Hard line."
A different kind of silence now. The silence of someone who understood exactly what those words meant.
"Chloe—"

"You've been in the field for weeks," Chloe said, and her voice was steady, absolutely steady, the voice of someone who had made a decision and would not unmake it. "You've carried this. You've been the one out there — the one visible, the one exposed, the one they're hunting. You've done your part, Maya. It's my turn."
"You've never done field work."
"I know."
"You don't—"

"I know what I don't know." Chloe zipped the backpack. The sound was final, a metallic rasp that seemed to cut through the encrypted connection. "But I know what I do know. I know the Lumina server better than anyone alive. I know the injection protocol because I wrote it. I know the production truck layout because I memorized the schematics. And I know that if I don't go, then on IPO day, four point two million phones light up and Vlad is still standing at that podium telling the world it's a hack. That's not enough. It's not enough to be right, Maya. We have to be undeniable."
Maya was quiet for a long time. When she spoke, her voice had changed — lower, stripped of argument, holding only something that might have been respect.
"Don't get caught."
"That's the plan."
"My plan was don't get caught too, and look where I am."
Chloe almost smiled. "You're still free. You're still fighting. That counts."
"Get to New York. Do the thing. And Chloe—"
"Yeah?"
"Make it hurt."
The line went dead.

Chloe stood in the center of the workspace, surrounded by the evidence of the last two weeks — the monitors she'd built Plan B on, the cables she'd used to test it, the empty cans that had fueled it. The room smelled of energy drink residue and the hot-dust scent of processors that had been running too long. She could still taste the Monster on her tongue — chemical, citrus, final.

She shouldered the backpack. It was heavier than she expected — not the weight of the hardware, but the weight of what it represented. She'd spent her whole war behind screens. Safe. Remote. The person you called when you needed the impossible made possible, delivered through fiber optics and radio waves. The architect, never the soldier.
Not anymore.
She killed the overhead lights. The workspace went dark except for the faint LED glow of the router she was leaving behind — a tiny green eye watching over the empty room.
Chloe Park walked out into the Oakland night.

The air hit her like a wall — cold, salt-tinged, carrying the distant sound of the harbor foghorn and the rumble of the 880 freeway. The streetlights on Telegraph Avenue buzzed at a frequency that settled somewhere between irritating and hypnotic. Her boots hit the sidewalk with a sound that was too loud, too physical, too real. Everything was louder out here. More present. More dangerous.

She'd spent seven weeks of this war surrounded by the hum of electronics, the click of keys, the glow of monitors. The world mediated through glass and light. Now the world was unfiltered — raw air, hard concrete, the knowledge that in six hours she'd be landing in a city where every federal agent and every one of Vlad's operatives would be watching for trouble.

They wouldn't be watching for her. That was the advantage of being the one who'd always stayed behind the screen. Nobody knew her face. Nobody knew her name. She was a ghost — and ghosts don't get caught.
The rideshare idled at the curb, headlights cutting through the fog. Chloe pulled open the back door, slid in, and said, "SFO. Terminal Two. As fast as you can."

The driver pulled away from the curb. Oakland shrank in the rear window — the warehouses, the street art, the darkened storefronts, the city where she'd built the weapon that would bring Vlad Kozlov down.
For the first time in this war, Chloe Park was not behind a screen.
She was on the front line.
And the Trojan horse was already inside the walls.
